claim_text: Rent control at the right design can improve tenure security without substantial supply penalty.
claim_notes: Democratic-socialist position defends second-generation rent stabilisation; framework predicts supply effects dominate.
hypothesis_claim: Binding rent control (price ceilings below market clearing with enforcement teeth) reduces aggregate housing supply in affected markets, reduces the quality of existing rent-controlled stock through reduced maintenance incentives, and produces distributional effects favouring long-tenure incumbents over mobile workers and new household formation. These effects are well-identified in the literature and represent a framework-validation opportunity: consensus among economists is ~93% that rent control reduces housing availability and quality (IGM Forum survey). If the framework cannot reproduce this on natural- experiment data (Berlin Mietendeckel 2020-2021, San Francisco 1994 expansion, Stockholm persistent control), it is broken.

covers_claims_entry.notes: Democratic-socialist defence of well-designed second-generation rent stabilisation is the contra-position; hypothesis predicts supply effects dominate across the three cases, which would falsify the claim.

error_messages: treatment_tags: claim treatment_tags ['second_generation_rent_stabilisation'] disjoint from hypothesis treatment_tags ['berlin_mietendeckel_2020', 'sf_rent_control_1994', 'stockholm_rent_control']
